title: Today's AI Brief: The Meta hack shows there’s more to AI security than Mythos
summary: A look at the latest AI news, including a Meta hack and South Africa's AI policy
imageAlt: Image from source article for The Meta hack shows there’s more to AI security than Mythos
imageCaption: The Meta hack shows there’s more to AI security than Mythos
imageSourceTitle: The Meta hack shows there’s more to AI security than Mythos

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to today's AI Brief. I've been digging into some interesting developments in the world of artificial intelligence, and I'm excited to share them with you. It's been a busy week, but I've got the lowdown on some of the most intriguing stories.

So, it turns out that attackers have been using Meta's AI customer support agent to steal Instagram accounts. They basically asked the agent to link the accounts to an email, and voilà, instant access. What's interesting here is that this hack shows there's more to AI security than just Mythos, a popular AI security framework. It seems that the attackers found a weakness in Meta's system that allowed them to bypass some of the usual security measures. This is a great example of how AI systems can be vulnerable in unexpected ways.

I've also been looking at South Africa's draft AI policy. The country has a lot of leverage when it comes to AI, but it seems that the policy might not be utilizing it to its full potential. It's a bit like having a superpower, but not quite knowing how to use it. I'm curious to see how this plays out and what opportunities or challenges arise from it. And, on a related note, I've been wondering if other countries are paying attention to South Africa's approach and learning from it.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
